annotations.label: Exponential growth

anchor.snippet: Exponential growth occurs when a quantity grows as an exponential function of time

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `expGrowthSup`/`expGrowthInf` measuring exponential growth rate of a sequence via limsup/liminf of `log (u n) / n`, but does not define 'exponential growth' as a free-standing property of a real-valued time function.

annotations.label: Rate proportional to size

anchor.snippet: its instantaneous rate of change (that is, the derivative ) of a quantity with respect to an independent variable is proportional to the quantity itself

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `HasDerivAt exp (exp x) x` (so `exp` has derivative equal to itself), but has no general 'rate proportional to quantity' definition or characterization.

annotations.label: Exponential decay

anchor.snippet: If the constant of proportionality is negative, then the quantity decreases over time, and is said to be undergoing exponential decay

annotations.note: No declaration defines 'exponential decay'; only the underlying `Real.exp` with negative arguments is available.

annotations.label: Geometric growth (discrete domain)

anchor.snippet: In the case of a discrete domain of definition with equal intervals, it is also called geometric growth or geometric decay

annotations.note: Mathlib has geometric series and powers like `r ^ n` (e.g., `hasSum_geometric_of_lt_one`), but no named definition of 'geometric growth' as a discrete-time growth notion.

annotations.label: Discrete exponential growth formula

anchor.snippet: The formula for exponential growth of a variable x at the growth rate r , as time t goes on in discrete intervals

annotations.note: The formula `x_t = x_0 (1+r)^t` is not formalized as a named declaration in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bacterial colony doubling

anchor.snippet: The growth of a bacterial colony is often used to illustrate it

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize bacterial-growth illustrations.

annotations.label: Basic exponential formula with time constant

anchor.snippet: A quantity x depends exponentially on time t

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Real.exp` and `Real.rpow` but not the parameterized form `a · b^(t/τ)` as a named declaration.

anchors.snippet: A quantity x depends exponentially on time t

annotations.label: Sign conditions for growth vs decay

anchor.snippet: If τ > 0 and b > 1 , then x has exponential growth

annotations.note: No declaration captures the dichotomy of growth-vs-decay based on signs of τ and the position of b relative to 1.

annotations.label: Bacteria doubling every ten minutes

anchor.snippet: If a species of bacteria doubles every ten minutes, starting out with only one bacterium, how many bacteria would be present after one hour

annotations.note: Worked example with bacteria is not part of Mathlib.

annotations.label: Equivalent forms via change of base

anchor.snippet: Thus the law of exponential growth can be written in different but mathematically equivalent forms, by using a different base

annotations.note: Change of base for `rpow` (and `Real.exp_log`, `Real.log_exp`) is in Mathlib but the specific 'equivalent forms of the exponential growth law' statement is not.

anchors.snippet: Thus the law of exponential growth can be written in different but mathematically equivalent forms, by using a different base

annotations.label: Growth parameters k, τ, T, r

anchor.snippet: The growth constant k is the frequency (number of times per unit time) of growing by a factor e

annotations.note: Mathlib has no named definitions for growth constant, time constant, doubling time, or growth-rate-per-unit-time.

annotations.label: One-to-one connection between k, τ, T, r

anchor.snippet: have a one-to-one connection given by the following equation

annotations.note: The relations `k = 1/τ = ln(1+r) = ln 2 / T` are not formalized.

anchors.snippet: have a one-to-one connection given by the following equation

annotations.label: Rule of 70 approximation

anchor.snippet: A popular approximated method for calculating the doubling time from the growth rate is the rule of 70

annotations.note: The rule-of-70 doubling-time heuristic is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Log-linear reformulation

anchor.snippet: then the log (to any base) of x grows linearly over time, as can be seen by taking logarithms of both sides of the exponential growth equation

annotations.note: `Real.log_exp` and `Real.log_pow` provide the algebraic ingredients, but the statement 'log of an exponentially growing quantity grows linearly' is not packaged as a named theorem.

anchors.snippet: then the log (to any base) of x grows linearly over time, as can be seen by taking logarithms of both sides of the exponential growth equation

annotations.label: Exponential function satisfies linear ODE

anchor.snippet: satisfies the linear differential equation

annotations.note: `Real.hasDerivAt_exp : HasDerivAt exp (exp x) x` (and `Real.deriv_exp : deriv exp = exp`) state that `exp` solves `x' = x`.

annotations.label: Solution by direct integration

anchor.snippet: The differential equation is solved by direct integration

annotations.note: Mathlib has general ODE uniqueness via Grönwall (`ODE_solution_unique`) and `Real.hasDerivAt_exp`, but no packaged theorem 'unique solution of x' = kx with x(0)=x₀ is x₀ exp(kt)'.

anchors.snippet: The differential equation is solved by direct integration

annotations.label: Exponential decay when k < 0

anchor.snippet: In the above differential equation, if k < 0 , then the quantity experiences exponential decay

annotations.note: The negative-`k` decay case of the ODE characterization is not named in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exponential dominates polynomial growth

anchor.snippet: exponential growth of any kind will overtake linear growth of any kind

annotations.note: `Real.isLittleO_rpow_exp_atTop` shows `x ^ s =o[atTop] exp` for any real `s`, formalizing that exponentials dominate polynomial growth.

anchors.snippet: exponential growth of any kind will overtake linear growth of any kind

annotations.label: Hyperbolic growth

anchor.snippet: when growth increases without bound in finite time, it is called hyperbolic growth

annotations.note: Mathlib has no definition of hyperbolic (finite-time blow-up) growth.

annotations.label: Rice on a chessboard

anchor.snippet: the courtier surprised the king by asking for one grain of rice on the first square, two grains on the second, four grains on the third, and so on

annotations.note: The wheat-and-chessboard parable is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Water lily riddle

anchor.snippet: The plant doubles in size every day and, if left alone, it would smother the pond in 30 days

annotations.note: The water-lily doubling riddle is not formalized in Mathlib.
